* a literal is *copy compatible* to type *T* if it may be copied to a value of type&nbsp;*T*
* type *T*<sub>*1*</sub> is *copy compatible* to type *T*<sub>*2*</sub> if an R&#8209;value of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*1*</sub> may be copied to an L&#8209;value of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*2*</sub>


### Compatibility, Passing ###

Parameter *passing compatibility* is a property that determines whether an entity may be passed to a formal parameter.

* a literal is *by-value passing compatible* to type *T* if it may be passed to a formal by&#8209;value parameter of type&nbsp;*T*
* a literal is *by-reference passing compatible* to type *T* if it may be passed to a formal `CONST`&nbsp;parameter of type *T*
* type *T*<sub>*1*</sub> is *by-value passing compatible* to type *T*<sub>*2*</sub> if a value of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*1*</sub> may be passed to a formal by&#8209;value parameter of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*2*</sub>
* type *T*<sub>*1*</sub> is *by-reference passing compatible* to type *T*<sub>*2*</sub> if a value of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*1*</sub> may be passed to a formal by&#8209;reference parameter of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*2*</sub>


### Compatibility, Expression ###

*Expression compatibility* is a property that determines whether an entity may be used together with another entity as operands in a binary expression.

* a literal is *expression compatible* to type *T* in respect of operation&nbsp;*M* if it may be used together with a value of type&nbsp;*T* as the operands in a binary expression with any operator representing operation&nbsp;*M*
* types *T*<sub>*1*</sub> and *T*<sub>*2*</sub> are *expression compatible* in respect of operation&nbsp;*M* if a value of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*1*</sub> and a value of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*2*</sub> may be used together as the operands in a binary expression with any operator representing operation&nbsp;*M*


### Constant Expression ###

A constant expression is an expression that is computable at compile time. A constant expression does not reference any variables nor invoke any library functions.


### Fractional Part ###

The *fractional part* of a real number *x* is the value that represents the digits of *x* after the decimal point if *x* was to be written out in decimal notation without an exponent.

Its definition is
* **frac** *x* = *x* - **int** *x*


### Integral Part ###

The *integral part* of a real number *x* is the value that represents the digits of *x* before the decimal point if *x* was to be written out in decimal notation without an exponent.

Its definition is
* **int** *x* = **sgn** *x* &middot; **entier** **abs** *x*


### Largest Integer or Entier ###

The *entier* value of a real number *x* is the largest integer less than *x*. Its definition is
* **entier** *x* = **max** *i* for *i* &in; { &#8484; | i < x }


### Length ###

The *length* of a character string *s* is the number of characters stored in *s* excluding its NUL terminator. Its definition is
* **length** *s* = **min** *n* for *n* &in; { &#8469; | *s*<sub>*n*</sub> = ASCII.NUL }

The *length* of an octet sequence *s* is the allocation size of the actual parameter *p* passed in to a formal parameter of type octet sequence. Its definition is
* **length** *s* = **tsize** *p* where *p* **passed-to** *s*


### Number Sets ###

In this specification abstract number sets are defined as follows:
* the set of the abstract natural numbers ℕ = { 0, 1, 2, 3, ... &infin; }
* the set of the abstract ordinal numbers 𝕆 = { 0, 1, 2, 3, ..., ω, ω+1, ω+2, ... }
* the set of the abstract whole numbers ℤ = { -&infin; ... -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, ... +&infin; }


### Odd Value ###

The *odd* value of a whole number *i* indicates whether *i* is odd. Its definition is
* **odd** *i* = ***true*** for *i* &in; { &#8484; | **abs** *i* **mod** 2 = 1 }
* **odd** *i* = ***false*** for *i* &in; { &#8484; | **abs** *i* **mod** 2 = 0 }

### Signum Value ###

The *signum* or ***sgn*** value of a signed number *x* indicates the sign of *x*. Its definition is
* **sgn** *x* = 1 for *x* &in; { &#8477; | *x* > 0 }
* **sgn** *x* = 0 for *x* &in; { &#8477; | *x* = 0 }
* **sgn** *x* = -1 for *x* &in; { &#8477; | *x* < 0 }


### Type, Any ###

A *type* is a classification of storage units defined by a set of three properties: storage representation, value range and semantics.

The set of all types is denoted *T*<sub>*any*</sub>.


### Type, Builtin ###

A *builtin type* is a type that is built into the language, that is, either a predefined type or a type provided by builtin module `UNSAFE`.

The set of all builtin types is denoted *T*<sub>*builtin*</sub>.


### Type, Countable ###

A *countable type* is a type that represents a set of values with a 1:1 correspondence to the first *m* ordinal numbers 𝕆 where *m* &in; 𝕆.
* **countable** *T* if &exist; *f* : *T* &rarr; 𝕆;

The set of all countable types is denoted *T*<sub>*countable*</sub>.
* &forall; **countable** *T*, *T* &in; *T*<sub>*countable*</sub>


### Type, Enumerated ###

An *enumerated type* is a type that represents a finite ordered set of identifiers.
* **enumerated** *T* if &forall; *e* &in; { *T* | **identifier** *e* } &and; &exist; *f* : *T* &rarr; &#8469;

The set of all enumerated types is denoted *T*<sub>*enum*</sub>.
* &forall; **enumerated** *T*, *T* &in; *T*<sub>*enum*</sub>


### Type, Machine ###

A *machine type* is a type that represents storage units that precisely match the storage units of the target architecture and have modular arithmetic semantics.

The set of all machine types is denoted *T*<sub>*machine*</sub>.


### Type, Signed ###

A *signed type* is a numeric type that represents both positive and negative scalar numeric values..
* **signed** *T* if &exist; *x*, *y* &in; { *T* | *x* < 0 &and; *y* > 0 }

The set of all signed types is denoted *T*<sub>*signed*</sub>.
* &forall; **signed** *T*, *T* &in; *T*<sub>*signed*</sub>


### Type, Unsigned ###

An *unsigned type* is a numeric type that represents non-negative scalar numeric values.
* **unsigned** *T* if &forall; *x* &in; { *T* | *x* &ge; 0 }

The set of all unsigned types is denoted *T*<sub>*unsigned*</sub>.
* &forall; **unsigned** *T*, *T* &in; *T*<sub>*unsigned*</sub>


### Type, Whole Number ###

A *whole number type* is a numeric type that represents a subset of the set of integers &#8484;.
* **whole** *T* if &forall; *n* &in; { *T* | *n* &in; &#8484; }

The set of all whole number types is denoted *T*<sub>*whole*</sub>.
* &forall; **whole** *T*, *T* &in; *T*<sub>*whole*</sub>


### Type, Real Number ###

A *real number type* is a numeric type that represents a subset of the set of real numbers &#8477;.
* **real** *T* if &forall; *r* &in; { *T* | *r* &in; &#8477; }

The set of all real number types is denoted *T*<sub>*real*</sub>.
* &forall; **real** *T*, *T* &in; *T*<sub>*real*</sub>


### Type, Cardinal ###

A *cardinal type* is a numeric type that represents a subset of the unsigned whole numbers &#8484;&#8314;.
* **cardinal** *T* if **unsigned** *T* &and; **whole** *T*

The set of all cardinal types is denoted *T*<sub>*cardinal*</sub>.
* &forall; **cardinal** *T*, *T* &in; *T*<sub>*cardinal*</sub>


### Type, Integer ###

An *integer type* is a numeric type that represents a subset of the signed whole numbers &#8484;.
* **integer** *T* if **signed** *T* &and; **whole** *T*

The set of all integer types is denoted *T*<sub>*integer*</sub>.
* &forall; **integer** *T*, *T* &in; *T*<sub>*integer*</sub>


### Type, Scalar ###

A *scalar type* is a numeric type that represents scalar values.

The set of all scalar types is denoted *T*<sub>*scalar*</sub>.


### Type, Collection ###

A *collection type* represents n-tuples of an arbitrary component type, where *n* is bounded by its capacity.

The set of all collection types is denoted *T*<sub>*collection*</sub>.


### Type, Array ###

An *array type* is an ordered collection type whose components are mapped to the set of the first *m* ordinal numbers 𝕆 where *m* is the [[capacity limit|Language-Description#capacity-limit]] of the array type.

The set of all array types is denoted *T*<sub>*array*</sub>.


### Type, String ###

An *string type* is an array type whose components are characters.

The set of all string types is denoted *T*<sub>*string*</sub>.


### Type, Record ###

The set of all record types is denoted *T*<sub>*record*</sub>.


### Type, Extensible Record ###

The set of all extensible record types is denoted *T*<sub>*extrec*</sub>.


### Type, Extensible Record Pointer ###

The set of all extensible record pointer types is denoted *T*<sub>*recptr*</sub>.

* *T*<sub>*recptr*</sub> = { *T* &in; *T*<sub>*pointer*</sub> &#8726; *T*<sub>*opaque*</sub> | **target-type** *T* &in; *T*<sub>*extrec*</sub> }


### Type, Opaque ###

The set of all opaque types is denoted *T*<sub>*opaque*</sub>.


### Type, Open Array ###

An *open array type* is a formal array type whose capacity is indeterminate at compile time.

The set of all open array types is denoted *T*<sub>*openarray*</sub>.


### Type, Variadic Parameter List ###

A *variadic parameter list* is a formal collection type whose cardinality is indeterminate at compile time.

The set of all variadic parameter lists is denoted *T*<sub>*arglist*</sub>.


### Value Count ###

The *value count* of an entity *e* is synonym with the [[cardinality|Language-Description#cardinality]] of *e*. Its definition is
* **count** *e* &equiv; **card** *e*, where **card** *e* is the cardinality of entity *e*


### Wirthian Macro ###

A *Wirthian macro* is a built-in syntactic entity whose occurrence is replaced with an appropriate library call, possibly filling in omitted arguments. The macro may be a statement or a built-in procedure or function. The mapped-to library must be explicitly imported.

Wirthian macros allow more convenient syntax than ordinary library calls and make a facility such as memory management or I/O&nbsp;appear to be built into the language even though it is actually provided by a library. Classic examples of Wirthian macros are procedures `NEW` and `DISPOSE` in earlier versions of Modula-2.

---

# Appendix A : Pragma Facilities
> This is the appendix headline

Pragmas are non-semantic directives &mdash; instructions to the language processor to control or influence the compilation process but do not alter the semantics of the compiling source code.

## Portable Pragma Specification

Modula-2 R10 incorporates the portable pragma specification for Modula-2 and other Wirthian languages at

[Portable Pragmas for Pascal, Modula-2 and Oberon](https://github.com/trijezdci/M2-Pragmas/wiki)

However, some of the pragmas specified do not apply to Modula-2 R10

[`RANGE`](https://github.com/trijezdci/M2-Pragmas/wiki/Pragma-Specification#range)
[`RELEASE`](https://github.com/trijezdci/M2-Pragmas/wiki/Pragma-Specification#release)
[`RETAIN`](https://github.com/trijezdci/M2-Pragmas/wiki/Pragma-Specification#retain)


## Mandatory Pragmas

The Bootstrap Kernel (BSK) subset requires support for the following pragmas

[`DEPRECATED`](https://github.com/trijezdci/M2-Pragmas/wiki/Pragma-Specification#deprecated)
[`FFI`](https://github.com/trijezdci/M2-Pragmas/wiki/Pragma-Specification#pragma-ffi)
[`FFIDENT`](https://github.com/trijezdci/M2-Pragmas/wiki/Pragma-Specification#pragma-ffident)
[`INLINE`](https://github.com/trijezdci/M2-Pragmas/wiki/Pragma-Specification#pragma-inline)
[`PRIVATETO`](https://github.com/trijezdci/M2-Pragmas/wiki/Pragma-Specification#pragma-privateto)

Implementations that support Unicode are further required to support pragma

[`ENCODING`](https://github.com/trijezdci/M2-Pragmas/wiki/Pragma-Specification#pragma-encoding)


## Implementation Defined Pragmas

Implementations may provide implementation defined pragmas in compliance with the portable pragma specification. The compiler ID prefix shall match `Impl.ShortName`. To facilitate portability, implementations that provide implementation defined pragmas shall further support pragmas

[`PRESETS`](https://github.com/trijezdci/M2-Pragmas/wiki/Pragma-Specification#pragma-presets)
[`UNSET`](https://github.com/trijezdci/M2-Pragmas/wiki/Pragma-Specification#pragma-unset)

---

# Appendix B : Managing Technical Debt #
> This is the appendix headline

Technical debt is a metaphor for the sum total consequences of improper (software) design and implementation. Such deficiencies may be unintentional, or they may well be intentional in an attempt to shorten time to delivery. In either case, resulting deficiencies tend to accumulate over time until they reach a critical point where further development and maintenance on the software is severely impaired.

The metaphor suggests – analogous to financial debt – that (1) technical debt has to be paid down over time and with interest, and (2) interest will grow exponentially if the debt is not paid down. If technical debt is left unchecked for too long, the software may become unmaintainable. Eventually, the time and cost to reduce the deficiencies to a manageable level may vastly outpace the time and cost to rewrite the software from scratch.

In many organisations it is next to impossible to obtain the resources required to do proper maintenance on larger software projects without quantifying the debt, its financial impact and the cost required to reduce it. But without documenting the technical debt first, it is impossible to obtain that data and present figures with any confidence. Documenting and reporting of technical debt is thus a necessary prerequisite to managing the debt.

## Documenting Debt ##

Modula-2 provides a facility to document technical debt directly within the source code. The facility is incorporated into the language syntax so that documented issues become machine readable for automated report generation. A reporting tool may then generate reports on the weight, quantity and location of technical debt within a code base directly from its source code. A simple report writer may be built into the language processor. For more complex analysis an external report writer may be used.

## Syntax ##

A `TO` `DO` list is a machine readable directive to document technical debt directly within the source code. It may be placed wherever a definition, a declaration or a statement can occur. It may be empty, or contain a tracking reference of an external issue tracking system, and a list of tasks with descriptions and effort estimations.

```antlr
toDoList := TO DO ( trackingRef? taskToDo ( ’;’ taskToDo )* END )? ;
trackingRef := ’(’ issueId ( ’,’ severity ’,’ description )? ’)’ ;
taskToDo := description ( ’,’ estimatedTime timeUnit )? ;
alias issueId, severity, estimatedTime = NumberLiteral ;
alias description = StringLiteral ;
alias timeUnit = StdIdent ; /* must be any of w, d, or h */
```

## Reporting Debt ##

The language processor must report any `TO` `DO` list with line number as an informational compile time message and any syntax violation within as a compile time error. It must further report the total number of lists found in a compilation unit. Any further analysis and reporting is implementation defined. It is recommended to implement more complex analysis and reporting as a separate reporting tool outside of the language processor.

## Examples ##

### TO DO without Arguments ###

The arguments may be omitted from a `TO` `DO` list altogether.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE TitleCaseTransform ( VAR s : ARRAY OF CHAR );
BEGIN
  FOR index, char IN s DO
    TO DO
  END (* FOR *)
END TitleCaseTransform;
```

### TO DO with Issue Tracking ###

A tracking reference of an external issue tracking system may be included in a `TO` `DO` list.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE skipBlockComment ( infile : Infile ) : Token;
  TO DO (1337, 2 (*minor*), "empty comment not recognised")
    "verify loop exit conditions", 1h;
    "fix and test", 1h
  END; (* TO DO *)
BEGIN
```

A tracking reference may refer to another at a different location while omitting its severity and description.

```Modula-2
TO DO (1337)
  "Fix: move increment to the end of the loop"
END; (* TO DO *)
```

### TO DO without Issue Tracking ###

The tracking reference may be omitted in a `TO` `DO` list.

```Modula-2
TYPE DynString = POINTER TO StringDescriptor;
TO DO
  "bindings to NEW and RETAIN", 2d;
  "bindings to _ATVALUE, _ATSTORE, _LENGTH", 2d;
  "bindings to APPEND, _ATINSERT, _ATREMOVE", 3d;
  "bindings to READ and WRITE, using template", 2d;
  "unit testing", 2d
END; (* TO DO *)
```

## Sample Report ##

```
$ m2tdr --summary --proj m2bsk
Modula-2 technical debt summary report for project m2bsk
found 14 issues with 39 tasks in 9 modules, thereof
   5 issues of weight 3 (major) with 23 tasks, est. 338 hours,
   7 issues of weight 2 (minor) with 12 tasks, est. 121 hours,
   2 issues of weight 1 (cosmetic) with 4 tasks, est. 19 hours;
found further 26 untracked tasks in 21 modules, est. 312 hours.
Total estimated effort for 65 tasks: 790 hours (avg. 12 hours/task).
```

---

# Appendix C : Atomic Operations #
> This is the appendix headline

Built-in module `ATOMIC` provides type-safe atomic operations. Its provision is **optional**.

### ATOMIC ###

```Modula-2
INTERFACE MODULE ATOMIC; (* OPTIONAL *)
(* Atomic Operations *)
TYPE OPERATION =
  ( SWAP,    (* atomic swap *)
    CAS,     (* atomic compare and swap *)
    INC,     (* atomic increment *)
    DEC,     (* atomic decrement *)
    BWNOT,   (* atomic bitwise NOT *)
    BWAND,   (* atomic bitwise AND *)
    BWNAND,  (* atomic bitwise NAND *)
    BWOR,    (* atomic bitwise OR *)
    BWXOR ); (* atomic bitwise XOR *)
PROCEDURE AVAIL ( op : OPERATION; bitwidth : CARDINAL ) : BOOLEAN;
(* Returns TRUE if the given operation is available for the given bitwidth,
   otherwise FALSE. The value of bitwidth must be a compile time expression. *)
PROCEDURE SWAP = ( VAR target : T; newVal : T ) : T;
(* Atomically writes newVal to target and returns the prior value of target.
   Both arguments must be of the same type. The return type matches the argument type. *)
PROCEDURE CAS ( VAR target : T; oldVal, newVal : T ) : T;
(* Atomically compares oldVal and target. If they match, newVal is written to
   target and oldVal is returned. Otherwise, the prior value of target is returned.
   All arguments must be of the same type. The return type matches the argument type. *)
PROCEDURE INC ( VAR target : T; increment : T ) : T;
(* Atomically increments target by increment and returns the prior value of target.
   Both arguments must be of the same type, which must be a built−in whole number type.
   The increment must be larger than zero. The returned type matches the target type. *)
PROCEDURE DEC ( VAR target : T; decrement : T ) : T;
(* Atomically decrements target by decrement and returns the prior value of target.
   Both arguments must be of the same type, which must be a built−in whole number type.
   The decrement must be larger than zero. The returned type matches the target type. *)
PROCEDURE BWNOT ( VAR target : T ) : T;
(* Atomically performs bitwise NOT of target, writes the result to target and returns the
   prior value of target. The argument may be of a built−in whole number type or a record
   with an octet sequence field. The returned type matches the target type. *)
PROCEDURE BWAND ( VAR target : T; mask : T ) : T;
(* Atomically performs bitwise AND of target and mask, writes the result to target and
   returns the prior value of target. The arguments may be of a built−in whole number type
   or a record with an octet sequence field. The returned type matches the target type. *)
PROCEDURE BWNAND ( VAR target : T; mask : T ) : T;
(* Atomically performs bitwise NAND of target and mask, writes the result to target and
   returns the prior value of target. The arguments may be of a built−in whole number type
   or a record with an octet sequence field. The returned type matches the target type. *)
PROCEDURE BWOR ( VAR target : T; mask : T ) : T;
(* Atomically performs bitwise OR of target and mask, writes the result to target and
   returns the prior value of target. The arguments may be of a built−in whole number type
   or a record with an octet sequence field. The returned type matches the target type. *)
PROCEDURE BWXOR ( VAR target : T; mask : T ) : T;
(* Atomically performs bitwise XOR of target and mask, writes the result to target and
   returns the prior value of target. The arguments may be of a built−in whole number type
   or a record with an octet sequence field. The returned type matches the target type. *)
END ATOMIC.
```
Functions of module `ATOMIC` may not be assigned nor passed as arguments to any procedure, function or macro.

---

# Appendix D : Syntax Diagrams #
> This is the appendix headline

## Non-Terminals ##

## Ignore Symbols ##

#### Whitespace ####
![Whitespace](https://github.com/m2sf/m2-bsk-syntax-diagrams/blob/master/png/Whitespace.png)

#### LineComment ####
![Line Comment](https://github.com/m2sf/m2-bsk-syntax-diagrams/blob/master/png/LineComment.png)

#### BlockComment ####
![Block Comment](https://github.com/m2sf/m2-bsk-syntax-diagrams/blob/master/png/BlockComment.png)

#### CommentCharacter ####
![Comment Character](https://github.com/m2sf/m2-bsk-syntax-diagrams/blob/master/png/CommentCharacter.png)

#### EndOfLine ####
![End Of Line Marker](https://github.com/m2sf/m2-bsk-syntax-diagrams/blob/master/png/EndOfLine.png)


### Pragmas ###

#### inlinePragma ####
![INLINE Pragma](https://github.com/m2sf/m2-bsk-syntax-diagrams/blob/master/png/inlinePragma.png)

#### deprecatedPragma ####
![DEPRECATED Pragma](https://github.com/m2sf/m2-bsk-syntax-diagrams/blob/master/png/deprecatedPragma.png)

#### privatetoPragma ####
![PRIVATETO Pragma](https://github.com/m2sf/m2-bsk-syntax-diagrams/blob/master/png/privatetoPragma.png)

#### ffiPragma ####
![FFI Pragma](https://github.com/m2sf/m2-bsk-syntax-diagrams/blob/master/png/ffiPragma.png)

#### ffidentPragma ####
![FFIDENT Pragma](https://github.com/m2sf/m2-bsk-syntax-diagrams/blob/master/png/ffidentPragma.png)

#### implDefPragma ####
![Implementation Defined Pragma](https://github.com/m2sf/m2-bsk-syntax-diagrams/blob/master/png/implDefPragma.png)

#### ctMsgMode ####
![Compile-Time Message Mode](https://github.com/m2sf/m2-bsk-syntax-diagrams/blob/master/png/ctMsgMode.png)

#### implPrefix ####
![Implementation Prefix](https://github.com/m2sf/m2-bsk-syntax-diagrams/blob/master/png/implPrefix.png)
